text: The surgeon displays a J-tube with an attached balloon, preparing to insert it laparoscopically. A small incision is made for the tube's placement. A catheter guide is used to assist in positioning the J-tube at the designated site.
The surgeon positions the J-tube at the intended site, using scissors to adjust its length for insertion into the small intestine, enhancing its rigidity for easier placement.

A laparoscopic J-tube is really just a pain in the butt, but in some patients that have had previous stomach surgery or have real bad aspiration, it's the only option. They don't really make a J-tube kit. So we kind of have to make this up as we go. So there. So that should be about, what's that, five, ten, fifteen centimeters from the area of concern. So that's good. So, scissors, cut it off. And then we will stick it inside here and then we'll see the rest later, Scott.

The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal segments of the jejunum, applying three small burns distally and one proximally. A hole is created in the jejunum, and the jejunostomy tube, equipped with a catheter guide, is inserted into the small intestine to facilitate placement.

The surgeon inflates the balloon at the balloon port to secure the small intestine to the abdominal wall, ensuring the jejunostomy tube is positioned distally. The balloon is larger than the hole in the intestine, preventing displacement. The surgeon then closes the hole around the tube using a purse string stitch, imbricating it to secure the tube. The tube is brought up to the abdominal wall, completing the laparoscopic jejunostomy tube placement.
